So Germany sees one nuclear plant have issues and decides to shut down EIGHT of their own reactors within two weeks. Because nothing says 'rational energy policy' like panic-shutting your entire nuclear infrastructure because someone else had an accident.

Surprise, surprise, when you eliminate 20% of your power generation overnight, suddenly everyone needs more natural gas. European demand spikes 15% faster than you can say 'maybe we should have thought this through first.'

Enter Putin, stage right, conveniently increasing pipeline capacity just when Europe desperately needs more gas. What are the odds that Russia would be perfectly positioned to fill Germany's self-created energy gap?

Ukraine's basically winning the lottery here, tripling their transit fees as all that sweet Russian gas flows through their Soviet-era pipes. Nothing like profiting off your neighbor's energy crisis while tensions with Russia are totally not escalating.

Now we're seeing steel production costs rise across Eastern Europe because energy got expensive. It's almost like shutting down reliable power sources has predictable economic consequences that ripple through manufacturing sectors.

A pierogi factory in Poland cuts weekend shifts and lays off twelve workers because energy costs are eating their margins. We've officially traced a nuclear accident in Japan to Polish dumpling unemployment in under two years.
